theme: Make It Personal — The Dashboard Should Remember You

ideas.title: Keyboard Shortcut Cheat Sheet Modal
ideas.description: The app has keyboard shortcuts (1=Office, 2=Dashboard, R=Refresh, Escape=blur) but ZERO discoverability. Users will never find them. Add a '?' key listener that opens a minimal modal listing all shortcuts. Implementation: a simple overlay with a grid of key→action pairs. Close on Escape or clicking outside. Add a tiny '⌨️' button in the header next to theme toggle that also opens it. WHY: Power users love shortcuts but only if they can discover them. A cheat sheet modal is the universal pattern (GitHub, Figma, Notion all do this). It takes 30 minutes to build and makes the app feel 10x more polished.

ideas.title: Activity Log Grouping by Time Blocks
ideas.description: The activity log is a flat reverse-chronological list. When there are 20 entries, it's a wall of text with no visual hierarchy. Group entries by time blocks: 'Just now' (< 5min), 'Earlier today', 'Yesterday', etc. Add a subtle date separator between groups — a thin line with the label centered. WHY: Every messaging app groups by date for a reason. Temporal grouping gives the log a narrative structure: 'what happened this morning vs this afternoon.' Without it, 20 entries blur together into noise.

ideas.title: Character Idle Animations — Unique Per Person
ideas.description: Idle workers all use the same wander animation paths. Each character should have a unique idle behavior that reflects their personality. Maya could doodle (small pencil animation near her), Carlos could type sporadically, Dave could bob his head to music (he has headphones!), Sofia could examine things with her magnifying glass. Implementation: add character-specific CSS classes like .char--idle-maya, .char--idle-dave with unique keyframe animations. Keep them subtle — a small looping gesture, not full character animation. WHY: Personality in idle states is what makes virtual offices feel alive. The Tamagotchi effect — people check in just to see what their favorites are doing. Right now idle workers are interchangeable; they should feel like individuals.

ideas.title: Worker Card Micro-Interactions — Hover States That Tell Stories
ideas.description: Worker cards have zero hover feedback beyond the default cursor. Add subtle hover states: card lifts slightly (translateY -2px, stronger shadow), the progress bar pulses once, and the status badge glows. On the error state, hovering could show a brief tooltip with the error context. CSS-only implementation: .worker-card:hover { transform: translateY(-2px); box-shadow: 0 8px 24px rgba(0,0,0,0.15); } .worker-card:hover .progress-bar-fill { animation: progress-hover-pulse 0.6s ease; }. WHY: Hover states are the first thing that separates 'student project' from 'polished product.' They signal interactivity and make the interface feel responsive. The card already flips on click — hover should hint that interaction is possible.

ideas.title: Roster Panel — Show the Full Team, Not Just Active Workers
ideas.description: The dashboard only shows active workers. If all 5 team members are idle/offline, you see an empty state. Add a collapsible 'Team Roster' section below the activity log showing ALL team members (from roster data) with their role, emoji, and last-seen time. Active members get a green dot; offline ones are grayed out. This gives visitors context about team composition even when nobody's working. WHY: A visitor landing when everyone is off-clock sees an empty page and bounces. A roster says 'this is a real team of 6 people, they're just not here right now.' It's the difference between an empty restaurant and a closed restaurant with a menu in the window.

ideas.title: Office Floor Weather Sync — Rain on the Window When It Rains IRL
ideas.description: The office window already shows time-of-day sky. Take it further: fetch real weather for the team's location and add weather effects. Rain drops on the window when it's raining, snow particles when it's snowing, lightning flashes during thunderstorms. Use wttr.in (no API key needed) with a 30-min cache. Fallback to clear sky on error. WHY: This is the kind of detail that makes people say 'wait, is it actually raining on their window right now?!' It bridges the virtual and physical worlds in a way that feels magical. The window is already there — it's begging for weather.

ideas.title: Slack-Style Status Messages — Let Workers Express Themselves
ideas.description: Add an optional 'status' field to workers.json beyond just working/idle/error. Workers could have custom status messages like '🎧 In deep focus', '☕ Coffee break', '🔥 Fixing prod', '📚 Reading docs'. Display as a small italic line under the worker name on both the card and the office floor character. The manager (Bloberto) sets these via workers.json updates. WHY: Status messages humanize workers. 'Working' is clinical. '🎧 In deep focus — do not disturb' tells a story. It's low-effort data (one extra JSON field) with high personality payoff.
